title: 12. Wet-lab handoff — three steps from frozen compute to first biological signal
lede: Every computational gate has been cleared. The remaining questions are about protein folding in cells, secretion efficiency, and functional binding to TMEM145 in vivo — none of which AF3 can answer. The path from here is physical: order DNA, clone a vector, co-transfect HEK293 cells, pull down, blot.
s1_body: Synthesise the CpG-depleted Ultra-Mini CDS (2,103 bp, 0 CpG, CAI 0.9635) as a gBlock fragment. No lab bench needed — ordered online with a credit card, shipped in two weeks. Sequence is finalised in cpg_depletion_ultra_mini_strc_max.fasta.
s1_deliverable: → 2,103 bp linear dsDNA in a tube
s2_body: Assemble the full cassette: 5′ITR · B8 enhancer · Kozak · IgK signal peptide · Ultra-Mini CDS · stop · WPRE3-compact · bGH polyA · 3′ITR. Outsource to VectorBuilder or GenScript with architecture spec; they handle synthesis, cloning, and QC.
s3_body: Co-transfect HEK293 cells with FLAG-tagged Ultra-Mini-STRC and HA-tagged TMEM145. Lyse, pull down with anti-FLAG beads, blot for HA. Positive signal confirms the AF3-predicted interface works in a mammalian membrane context. Derstroff et al. ran this exact assay for native STRC and got a positive result; we aim to replicate with the Ultra-Mini construct.
s3_deliverable: → Western blot: does HA-TMEM145 co-precipitate with FLAG-Ultra-Mini?
footer: Beyond Step 3 the pathway continues: mouse model transduction (~$20–50k, 6–12 months) · IND-enabling toxicology ($500k–2M) · Phase 1 trial ($5–20M). The compute-to-clinic gap closes at Step 3 — everything before it is argumentation, everything after it is pharmacology.
